label: Tense choice (diagnostic-only)
description: Cross-cutting bucket family for items where the only diagnostic is choosing the right tense, not forming it. All items present fully-formed verbs and ask the learner to pick. Covers six contrast areas: imperfetto vs passato prossimo (cross-referenced from the imperfect.discrimination tree, not duplicated here), progressive vs simple, future vs present-for-future, conditional vs imperfetto in counterfactuals, indicative vs subjunctive, and trapassato vs imperfetto. Items dispatched to this topic deliberately do NOT cite formation buckets so the signal stays pure.

attributes.cross_reference: Items for imperfetto vs passato prossimo cite verb_form.imperfect.discrimination.* directly; no buckets duplicated here for that area.

label: Progressive vs simple form (sto parlando vs parlo)
description: Italian's progressive (stare + gerundio) is used more sparingly than English's 'be + -ing'. Most English progressives map to a simple Italian tense. The progressive in Italian is reserved for action genuinely-in-progress-right-now or a strongly-emphasised ongoing action. Over-using the progressive is a sleeper miss for English speakers.

label: Present progressive (sto parlando) vs present (parlo)
description: Italian usually expresses 'I'm doing X' with the simple present (parlo), reserving the progressive (sto parlando) for emphasis on right-now in-progress action. 'What are you doing?' is 'Che fai?' more often than 'Che stai facendo?'. Habits, dispositions, and ongoing states all use the simple present.

canonical_examples: Che fai? (default) vs Che stai facendo? (emphatic right-now)
Mangio la pasta (habit) vs sto mangiando la pasta (right now)
attributes.common_miss: over-applying the progressive for any English -ing form

label: Past progressive (stavo parlando) vs imperfetto (parlavo)
description: Italian usually expresses 'I was doing X' with the imperfetto (parlavo), reserving the past progressive (stavo parlando) for vivid emphasis on the right-then in-progress action. The imperfetto already carries the ongoing-action reading; the progressive is an intensifier, not the default.

canonical_examples: Mentre dormivo (default) vs mentre stavo dormendo (vivid emphasis)
attributes.common_miss: over-applying the past progressive for any English 'was -ing'

label: Future vs present-for-future (parlerò vs parlo)
description: Italian uses the simple present for many near-future events, especially when a time adverb anchors the future reference ('parto domani'). The morphological future (parlerò) is used for less-certain, more-distant, or emphatically-projected futures, plus for probability ('saranno le tre' = 'it must be three o'clock').

label: Future for unambiguous future (parlerò)
description: When the future is genuinely uncertain, emphatically projected, or distant ('one day I will speak Italian fluently'), Italian uses the morphological future. Also for probability or supposition: 'saranno le tre', 'avrà trent'anni'.

attributes.common_miss: using present for cases where future is required (distant, uncertain, or probabilistic)

label: Present for near-future (parto domani)
description: When a time adverb makes the future reference clear, Italian commonly uses the simple present: 'parto domani', 'arriviamo alle otto', 'l'anno prossimo cambio lavoro'. Doing this in English would feel wrong ('I leave tomorrow' is marked), but in Italian it's the default for definite near-future plans.

attributes.common_miss: over-applying the morphological future when a present-for-future is more natural

label: Conditional vs imperfetto in counterfactuals
description: Counterfactual 'if X, then Y' sentences have a prescriptive form (subjunctive in the protasis + conditional in the apodosis: 'se avessi tempo, verrei') and a widespread colloquial form (imperfetto in both: 'se avevo tempo, venivo'). Both are heard in modern Italian. The prescriptive form is required in writing; the colloquial form is common in speech.

label: Prescriptive: subjunctive + conditional (se avessi tempo, verrei)
description: The textbook counterfactual: imperfect subjunctive in the protasis (the 'se' clause), conditional in the apodosis. Used in writing and in careful speech. The learner who has internalised it should be able to produce it on demand.

attributes.canonical_example: Se avessi tempo, verrei alla festa
attributes.common_miss: using present indicative + future ('se ho tempo, verrò') which is fine for a real condition but wrong for a counterfactual

label: Colloquial: imperfetto + imperfetto (se avevo tempo, venivo)
description: Widespread spoken counterfactual: imperfetto in both protasis and apodosis. Prescriptively dispreferred but heard everywhere. Worth recognising for comprehension and being able to produce in informal contexts.

attributes.canonical_example: Se avevo tempo, venivo alla festa
attributes.common_miss: treating the colloquial form as wrong when in fact it's accepted in speech

label: Indicative vs subjunctive in subordinates
description: Italian's subjunctive is triggered by certain matrix verbs and conjunctions. The diagnostic is whether the trigger forces the subjunctive or not. Modern colloquial Italian often uses indicative where prescriptive Italian wants subjunctive, especially after opinion verbs ('penso che è' instead of 'penso che sia'). Both registers are worth recognising.

label: Opinion verbs (penso che, credo che, mi sembra che)
description: Verbs of personal opinion or belief prescriptively trigger the subjunctive in their complement clause. 'Penso che sia vero' (prescriptive) vs 'penso che è vero' (colloquial). Both are heard; the subjunctive is required in writing and signals more education in speech.

attributes.common_miss: using indicative ('penso che è'), accepted in speech but not in writing

label: Emotion verbs (sono contento che, mi dispiace che)
description: Expressions of emotion or feeling about the subordinate clause's content trigger the subjunctive. 'Sono contento che tu sia qui' (not 'sei qui'). Less optional than opinion verbs; the indicative is heard but feels markedly wrong.

canonical_examples: Sono contento che tu sia qui
Mi dispiace che lui non possa venire
attributes.common_miss: using indicative ('sei qui'), more clearly wrong than after opinion verbs

label: Hypothetical conjunctions (sebbene, benché, prima che, affinché)
description: Conjunctions introducing concession, time-before, or purpose require the subjunctive. The trigger is structural (the conjunction), not lexical. 'Sebbene piova, esco' (not 'piove'). Stronger than opinion triggers; almost no colloquial slippage here.

canonical_examples: Prima che tu parta, voglio dirti una cosa
attributes.common_miss: using indicative after these conjunctions, much rarer in speech than after opinion verbs

label: Negation triggering subjunctive (non penso che, non è vero che)
description: Negating a verb that doesn't ordinarily trigger the subjunctive can flip it. 'È vero che piove' (indicative) vs 'non è vero che piova' (subjunctive). Same with 'non penso che sia' vs the affirmative 'penso che' (which itself triggers subjunctive in prescriptive Italian). The diagnostic is whether the polarity has changed and whether the trigger applies under negation.

canonical_examples: È vero che piove vs non è vero che piova
attributes.common_miss: keeping indicative under negation when subjunctive is now triggered

label: Trapassato prossimo vs imperfetto (had done vs was doing)
description: Both are past-of-past tenses but with different aspects. The trapassato (avevo mangiato) marks a completed event prior to another past event. The imperfetto (mangiavo) marks an ongoing action or state at the time of another past event. Picking between them when both are grammatically possible is the diagnostic.

label: Trapassato for completed-prior-to-prior (quando sono arrivato, aveva già mangiato)
description: When the second past event was already complete by the time of the first past reference point, Italian uses the trapassato: 'Quando sono arrivato, lui aveva già mangiato' (he had already eaten). The 'already' adverbs (già, appena, da poco) cue this strongly.

canonical_examples: Quando sono arrivato, aveva già mangiato
Mi ha detto che era appena uscita
attributes.common_miss: using imperfetto for a clearly-completed prior event ('mangiava' for 'had eaten')

label: Imperfetto for ongoing background (mangiavo quando sei entrato)
description: When the second past event was still ongoing at the time of the first past reference point, Italian uses the imperfetto: 'Mangiavo quando sei entrato' (I was eating when you came in). The 'while' or 'when -ing' adverbs (mentre, quando) cue this strongly.

canonical_examples: Mentre dormivo, è suonato il telefono
attributes.common_miss: using trapassato for an ongoing state ('avevo mangiato quando sei entrato' implies I'd finished, which usually isn't the intended reading)

label: Present vs imperfect
description: Choosing the present or the imperfect where the frame could read either way. Marginal as a direct choice; the cue is whether the routine/description sits in present time or past time.

label: Present vs imperfect habitual
description: Present for a current routine (ogni giorno passa di qui) vs imperfetto for a past routine (ogni giorno passava di qui). The cue is the time frame, not the verb.

attributes.canonical_example: ogni giorno passa di qui / passava di qui

label: Present vs passato prossimo
description: Choosing the present or the passato prossimo where English would reach for a perfect. The classic trap is the durative da construction, where Italian uses the present for an action ongoing since a past point.

label: Present with da for ongoing-since
description: Italian uses the PRESENT (not the perfect) for an action continuing from a past point: Studio italiano da due anni. English 'I have studied' tempts the passato prossimo.

attributes.canonical_example: Vivo qui da dieci anni (not ho vissuto)

label: Passato prossimo for a completed event
description: A finished, bounded event takes the passato prossimo, not the present: Ieri ho mangiato. The counterweight to the durative present.

label: Passato remoto vs passato prossimo
description: Choosing between the passato remoto and the passato prossimo for a completed past event. Largely a register and regional choice rather than a strict aspectual rule.

label: Passato remoto for literary / historical narration
description: The passato remoto is the default narrative past in writing, literature, history (and southern speech): Dante nacque nel 1265. Detached from present relevance.

attributes.canonical_example: Il re morì e il regno cadde

label: Passato prossimo for spoken / present-relevant past
description: Everyday speech (especially northern / standard) uses the passato prossimo for past events, including remote ones, when conversational or present-relevant: Sono nato nel 1990.

label: Dare: present or future both fine
description: Union bucket for question frames where dai and darai are both natural; the diagnostic is producing dare correctly at all, not the tense choice. Ratified 2026-05-28 (pronoun granularity thread).
